formula_visual: [NOUN] + who (people) / which (things) / that (people or things) + [extra information about the noun]
when_to_use: Use relative clauses to add information about a noun without starting a new sentence. 'Who' refers to people, 'which' refers to things, and 'that' can refer to either (common in informal speech and DEFINING/essential clauses).
when_to_use_ar: استخدم جمل الصلة لإضافة معلومة عن اسم بدون بدء جملة جديدة. who للأشخاص، which للأشياء، that للاتنين (شائعة في الكلام العادي والجمل التعريفية الأساسية).
why_arabic_speakers_struggle: Arabic relative clauses use الذي/التي/اللي, which don't map one-to-one onto who/which/that by grammatical category (person vs. thing) — Arabic's system is more driven by gender/number agreement. Learners often use 'which' for people or 'who' for things, and frequently forget to drop the relative pronoun in casual object-relative clauses where English allows it ('the book I read' vs. always saying 'the book that I read').

examples.en: The woman who called you is my sister.
This is the book which changed my life.
examples.structure: [This is the book] + [which changed my life]

examples.en: The restaurant that we visited was amazing.
The book (that) I read last week was excellent.
examples.ar: الكتاب اللي قريته الأسبوع اللي فات كان ممتاز (that ممكن تُحذف هنا)
examples.structure: [The book] + [(that) I read last week] + [was excellent] — 'that' optional here since it's the object of the clause

examples.en: My brother, who lives in Canada, is visiting next month.
examples.ar: أخويا، اللي عايش في كندا، جاي زيارة الشهر الجاي (معلومة إضافية غير أساسية، بين فواصل)
examples.structure: [My brother], [who lives in Canada], [is visiting next month] — non-defining, adds extra (non-essential) info

common_errors.wrong: The woman which called you is my sister.
common_errors.correct: The woman who called you is my sister.
common_errors.explanation: 'which' refers to things, not people — use 'who' (or 'that') for a person.

common_errors.wrong: This is the book who I read.
common_errors.correct: This is the book that/which I read.
common_errors.explanation: 'who' refers to people, not things — use 'which' or 'that' for a book.

common_errors.wrong: My brother, that lives in Canada, is visiting.
common_errors.correct: My brother, who lives in Canada, is visiting.
common_errors.explanation: 'that' is not used in non-defining clauses (the ones set off by commas, adding extra non-essential information) — only 'who'/'which' are used there.

practice_fill_blank.sentence: The man ___ won the award is my colleague.
The car ___ I bought last year broke down.
My sister, ___ is a doctor, lives in London.
This is the movie ___ everyone is talking about.
practice_fill_blank.hint: Refers to a thing, defining clause.

practice_fill_blank.sentence: The company, ___ was founded in 1990, is very successful.
practice_fill_blank.hint: Non-defining clause about a thing — 'that' is not allowed here.

quick_rule: WHO = people. WHICH = things. THAT = either, but ONLY in defining (essential, no-comma) clauses — never after a comma. When the relative pronoun is the OBJECT of its clause, it can often be dropped entirely in casual speech.
quick_rule_ar: WHO للأشخاص. WHICH للأشياء. THAT للاتنين بس في الجمل التعريفية الأساسية (بدون فاصلة).
mnemonic: WHO = people you know WHO they are. WHICH one of these THINGS? THAT can replace either one, but never after a comma pause.
connect_to_speaking: Practice with your buddy: describe 3 people or things important to you using relative clauses (e.g., 'My best friend, who I've known for 10 years, ...' or 'The city that I grew up in ...').
